initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoc in chemical engineering at Hanyang University, I see the BK21 program's budget allocation directly from the policy context: the Ministry of Education provides about 408,080 million KRW annually for education research groups, plus 52,896 million KRW for graduate innovation support. For Hanyang, the total funding allocated is a share of that national budget. Based on the policy's resource logic, graduate stipends are set at 700K-1.3M KRW per month, and postdoc minimum salary is 3M KRW per month. Given Hanyang's size and typical number of education research groups (around 20-30 groups in science/engineering), I estimate roughly 1,200 graduate scholarship slots (combining master's, doctoral, and doctoral candidate slots) and about 150 postdoc positions funded annually. This aligns with the national scale of about 12,600 graduate students per year in future talent training and 6,400 in innovation talent training, scaled to Hanyang's share.
evidence: The policy states the annual budget for education research groups is 408,080 million KRW nationally, and Hanyang University hosts multiple education research groups in science/engineering fields, so its share is substantial.

Graduate stipend levels (700K-1.3M KRW/month) and postdoc minimum salary (3M KRW/month) are fixed, so the number of slots is determined by dividing the group budget by these amounts, after accounting for operating costs (limited to 10%).

Eligibility criteria limit the number of education research groups per university based on field and past performance; Hanyang likely has 20-30 groups, each supporting 40-60 graduate students and 5-10 postdocs, yielding my estimates.

initial_posts.narrative: As a master's student in materials engineering at a national university in Seoul, I am directly supported by the BK21 program and see the outputs phase as the direct result of the inputs and activities. With 1,150 graduate scholarship slots and 145 postdoc positions funded, and funds disbursed on schedule, the research groups are active. Based on my own group's productivity and discussions with peers, I estimate that each graduate student produces about 2-3 publications per year on average, and each postdoc produces about 3-4. Given that not all students are equally productive, I conservatively assume an average of 2.5 publications per graduate student and 3.5 per postdoc, yielding roughly 2,875 from graduate students and 507 from postdocs, totaling about 3,382. I round to 3,450 to account for some collaborative publications. For international conference presentations, I estimate about 0.5 per graduate student and 1 per postdoc, based on my experience that about half of students present at an international conference each year, while postdocs often present more. That gives 575 from graduate students and 145 from postdocs, totaling 720, but I round down to 690 because some presentations may be at domestic conferences. I have not seen other participants' posts yet, so these are my independent estimates based on my personal experience in a materials engineering lab.
evidence: In my own research group of about 40 graduate students and 5 postdocs, we produce roughly 80-100 publications per year, which scales to about 2-3 per graduate student and 3-4 per postdoc, supporting my average estimates.

From conversations with peers in other groups at Hanyang, I know that productivity varies but the average is around 2.5 publications per graduate student per year, consistent with my group's experience.

International conference attendance is common but not universal; about half of graduate students in my department present at an international conference each year, and postdocs often present at one or two, supporting my estimate of 0.5 per graduate student and 1 per postdoc.

The mandatory reporting and compliance checks ensure that most groups submit their data, so I trust that the outputs I estimate are reasonably accurate based on the active cohort of 1,150 graduate students and 145 postdocs in 2022.
